title: Tensor Calculus of Curves and Surfaces â€” Mermaid-Safe v2
description: Beginner lesson on parametrized curves, parametrized surfaces, the first fundamental form, and the second fundamental form.

sections.label: Â§1 Curves to Surfaces
sections.title: Curves to Surfaces
sections.content:
Curves to Surfaces

A curve has one parameter. A surface has two parameters. Tensor calculus begins when we stop thinking only about points and start asking how measurements change when parameters change.

Definition
A parametrized curve is a map
$$\gamma:I\subset\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}^3,\qquad t\mapsto \gamma(t).$$
The velocity vector is $\gamma'(t)$, and the speed is $\|\gamma'(t)\|$.
Curve metric
For a curve, the infinitesimal squared length is
$$ds^2=\langle \gamma'(t),\gamma'(t)\rangle\,dt^2.$$
This is the one-dimensional version of the first fundamental form.

Curve idea

A curve has one direction of motion. A tiny parameter change $dt$ produces the tiny displacement $d\gamma=\gamma'(t)dt$.

$$\|d\gamma\|^2=\langle \gamma',\gamma'\rangle dt^2.$$

Surface idea

A surface has two coordinate directions. A tiny parameter change $(du,dv)$ produces the tiny displacement $dX=X_u\,du+X_v\,dv$.

$$\|dX\|^2=\langle dX,dX\rangle.$$
Reader checkpoint
If a formula contains a dot product, it is probably measuring length or angle. The first fundamental form is built out of dot products of tangent vectors.

sections.label: Â§2 Parametric Surfaces
sections.title: Parametric Surfaces
sections.content:
Parametric Surfaces

A parametrized surface is a map with two parameters. We write it as $X(u,v)$, where $(u,v)$ are coordinates on the parameter plane.

Definition
A parametrized surface is
$$X:U\subset\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^3,\qquad (u,v)\mapsto X(u,v).$$
The two coordinate tangent vectors are
$$X_u=\frac{\partial X}{\partial u},\qquad X_v=\frac{\partial X}{\partial v}.$$
Regular surface test
The parametrization is regular at a point if
$$X_u\times X_v\neq 0.$$
This means the two tangent directions do not collapse into a single line.
Example
The graph of $z=f(u,v)$ has parametrization
$$X(u,v)=(u,v,f(u,v)).$$
Then
$$X_u=(1,0,f_u),\qquad X_v=(0,1,f_v).$$
For $X(u,v)=(u,v,u^2+v^2)$, enter $X_u$.

sections.label: Â§3 First Fundamental Form
sections.title: First Fundamental Form
sections.content:
The First Fundamental Form

The first fundamental form is the rule that computes lengths, angles, and areas on a surface by using the dot products of $X_u$ and $X_v$.

Definition
The coefficients of the first fundamental form are
$$E=\langle X_u,X_u\rangle,\qquad F=\langle X_u,X_v\rangle,\qquad G=\langle X_v,X_v\rangle.$$
The quadratic form is
$$I=ds^2=E\,du^2+2F\,du\,dv+G\,dv^2.$$
Matrix form
With $d\mathbf{u}=\begin{bmatrix}du\\dv\end{bmatrix}$, the same object is
$$ds^2=d\mathbf{u}^{T}\begin{bmatrix}E&F\\F&G\end{bmatrix}d\mathbf{u}.$$
The matrix $g_{ij}=\langle X_i,X_j\rangle$ is the metric tensor in these coordinates.
QuantityFormulaMeaning
$E$$X_u\cdot X_u$length-squared of the $u$ coordinate direction
$F$$X_u\cdot X_v$angle interaction between the two coordinate directions
$G$$X_v\cdot X_v$length-squared of the $v$ coordinate direction
$EG-F^2$$\|X_u\times X_v\|^2$area scaling squared

Formula for a graph

For $X(u,v)=(u,v,f(u,v))$:

$$E=1+f_u^2,\quad F=f_u f_v,\quad G=1+f_v^2.$$

Paraboloid example

If $f(u,v)=a(u^2+v^2)$, then

$$E=1+4a^2u^2,\quad F=4a^2uv,\quad G=1+4a^2v^2.$$
For $X(u,v)=(u,v,a(u^2+v^2))$, enter $E$.

sections.label: Â§4 Second Fundamental Form
sections.title: Second Fundamental Form
sections.content:
The Second Fundamental Form

The first fundamental form measures inside the tangent plane. The second fundamental form measures how the surface bends away from that tangent plane.

Unit normal
For a regular parametrized surface, define the unit normal vector by
$$N=\frac{X_u\times X_v}{\|X_u\times X_v\|}.$$
The normal vector points perpendicular to the tangent plane.
Second fundamental form
The coefficients of the second fundamental form are
$$e=\langle X_{uu},N\rangle,\qquad f=\langle X_{uv},N\rangle,\qquad g=\langle X_{vv},N\rangle.$$
The quadratic form is
$$II=e\,du^2+2f\,du\,dv+g\,dv^2.$$
Interpretation
The first form says how a short vector appears to someone living on the surface. The second form says how much the surface bends in the normal direction when moving along that short vector. 
flowchart LR
  A["Parametrization"] --> B["Tangent vectors"]
  B --> C["First form"]
  B --> D["Normal vector"]
  D --> E["Second derivatives"]
  E --> F["Second form"]
  C --> G["Length and angle"]
  F --> H["Bending"]
Graph surface formula
For $X(u,v)=(u,v,f(u,v))$,
$$N=\frac{(-f_u,-f_v,1)}{\sqrt{1+f_u^2+f_v^2}}.$$
Therefore
$$e=\frac{f_{uu}}{\sqrt{1+f_u^2+f_v^2}},\qquad f=\frac{f_{uv}}{\sqrt{1+f_u^2+f_v^2}},\qquad g=\frac{f_{vv}}{\sqrt{1+f_u^2+f_v^2}}.$$
For $f(u,v)=a(u^2+v^2)$, enter $e$ at $(u,v)=(0,0)$.

sections.label: Â§5 Shape Operator and Curvature
sections.title: Shape Operator and Curvature
sections.content:
Shape Operator and Curvature

The first and second forms are often combined. Their ratio in a tangent direction gives normal curvature.

Normal curvature
For a tangent displacement $w=(du,dv)$, the normal curvature in that direction is
$$k_n(w)=\frac{II(w,w)}{I(w,w)}=\frac{e\,du^2+2f\,du\,dv+g\,dv^2}{E\,du^2+2F\,du\,dv+G\,dv^2}.$$
Tensor notation
Using indices $i,j\in\{1,2\}$, write
$$I=g_{ij}\,du^i du^j,\qquad II=b_{ij}\,du^i du^j.$$
Here $g_{ij}=X_i\cdot X_j$ and $b_{ij}=X_{ij}\cdot N$.
Proof sketch
Since $dX=X_u\,du+X_v\,dv$, taking the dot product with itself gives
$$dX\cdot dX=(X_u\cdot X_u)du^2+2(X_u\cdot X_v)dudv+(X_v\cdot X_v)dv^2.$$
This is exactly the first fundamental form.

sections.label: Â§6 Examples and Tables
sections.title: Examples and Tables
sections.content:
Examples and Tables

This section compares a plane, a sphere, and a graph surface. The formulas are simplified to make the structural pattern visible.

SurfaceParametrizationFirst form ideaSecond form idea
Plane$X(u,v)=(u,v,0)$$E=G=1$, $F=0$$e=f=g=0$
Sphere$X(\theta,\phi)$depends on radius and latitudenonzero bending everywhere
Graph$X(u,v)=(u,v,f(u,v))$depends on $f_u,f_v$depends on $f_{uu},f_{uv},f_{vv}$

sections.label: Â§7 Export Layout and Review
sections.title: Export Layout and Review
sections.content:
Export Layout and Review

This section includes explicit layout controls so that Print/PDF and Reveal export can be tested.

Keep together
This example block should stay together in Print/PDF when possible.
$$I=E\,du^2+2F\,du\,dv+G\,dv^2.$$

The comment above is a Reveal slide split marker.

This paragraph is inside an allow-break block. It is intentionally verbose: first fundamental form, metric tensor, tangent basis, coordinate grid, second fundamental form, normal vector, curvature, shape operator, and normal curvature. This is a safe place to permit a page break if the printed output needs one.

This paragraph follows a forced Print/PDF page break.

ObjectInputOutputUsed forBeginner interpretation
$I$$X_u,X_v$$E,F,G$length/angle/areaintrinsic measuring rule
$II$$X_{uu},X_{uv},X_{vv},N$$e,f,g$bendingnormal direction bending rule
$k_n$$I,II,w$numberdirectional curvaturebending along one direction
Study task
Explain in one sentence why the first fundamental form can measure lengths without leaving the surface.

quiz.stem: What does the first fundamental form primarily measure?
options: Length and angle on the surface
quiz.explanation: The first form is the metric rule on the surface.

quiz.stem: The coefficient F is the dot product X_u Â· X_v.
Enter the first fundamental form matrix using E,F,G.
quiz.hint: Put E and G on the diagonal and F off the diagonal.

quiz.stem: For the plane X(u,v)=(u,v,0), enter E.
What does the second fundamental form measure?
options: Bending relative to the normal vector
quiz.explanation: The second form uses second derivatives and the unit normal.

quiz.stemHtml: For a graph surface $X(u,v)=(u,v,f(u,v))$, the normal vector depends on $f_u$ and $f_v$.
quiz.explanation: The unit normal is proportional to (-f_u,-f_v,1).

quiz.hint: Differentiate first with respect to u, then v.

quiz.stem: In your own words, distinguish I from II.
quiz.sampleAnswer: I measures lengths and angles in the tangent plane; II measures bending in the normal direction.
